### Note: This review is auto-generated with insufficient certainty, I do NOT think these all opinions are valid.

Can you provide a compact, self-contained description (possibly as a lemma with proof) of the splice permutation used in the even case, together with a brief argument proving it is a single cycle, without requiring readers to consult long tables?
The need for the extra affine defect family when m ≡ 4 (mod 6) is explained heuristically via “residue-3 strands.” Can you formalize this obstruction and its resolution, e.g., by stating and proving a short proposition that isolates exactly why boundary-only fixes cannot work?
Would it be possible to include a small computational verification (e.g., Sage/Python code) that constructs the three Hamilton cycles produced by Route E and verifies arc-disjointness and Hamiltonicity for a range of m, say up to 50? This would help readers trust the intricate casework.
Does your sign-product invariant extend to more than three colors (e.g., to colorings with k permutations summing to fixed generators), and could it produce analogous parity obstructions in higher-dimensional directed tori or other Cayley digraphs?
Can your approach be extended to higher dimensions, e.g., D_d(m) = C⃗_m^□d for d ≥ 4? Are there conceptual obstacles (parity or otherwise) that arise for certain parities of m and d?
In the odd case, are the five Kempe swaps minimal in any meaningful sense (e.g., number of plane/line supports or number of swaps) to obtain a Hamilton decomposition from the canonical coloring?
For readability, could you add a figure showing P0 in (i,k)-coordinates, indicating the bulk moves and the defect lines/points for each color, at least for a representative even m (e.g., m = 10)?

This is a strong and timely contribution. The main theorem settles a natural and nontrivial decomposition problem for the directed 3-torus across all m ≥ 3. The odd case is clean, explicit, and easily verifiable. The parity obstruction for even m is insightful, and the Route E construction is explicit and conceptually guided by a well-articulated return-map framework. While the even-case analysis is necessarily more technical, the authors organize the argument around bulk dynamics with bounded-defect structure and a first-return counting lemma, which offers a clear mechanism to conclude single-cycle behavior once the defect bookkeeping is done. I recommend publication pending minor revisions focused on (i) ensuring all appendices provide complete, accessible proofs of the piecewise formulas and the finite splice, (ii) clarifying the m ≡ 4 (mod 6) obstruction and its fix, and (iii) improving exposition (figures/diagrams) and correcting small typographical artifacts. With these adjustments, the paper will be a valuable addition to the Journal of Graph Theory.
